title: Pig herd health programme and vaccination calendar
subtitle: Planning a basic health schedule for a small outdoor pig herd.
excerpt: A small pig herd that moves through weaners each year needs a consistent health programme, worming, vaccination, and observation, to stay productive and to meet the recording requirements that come with keeping pigs. Here's what a basic calendar looks like for an outdoor smallholder keeping one or two sows or a series of bought-in weaners.
sourceNotes: DEFRA/APHA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Pigs. AHDB Pork, herd health planning. UK Veterinary Medicines Regulations 2013, medicine records for food-producing animals.

glossaryTerms.definition: A written agreement between a vet and a livestock keeper setting out the health management, vaccination, and treatment programme for the holding.
A bacterial disease of pigs (Erysipelothrix rhusiopathiae) causing skin diamond lesions, joint inflammation, and sudden death if untreated; prevented by vaccination.

content.text: with your vet is a legal requirement for any pig holding in England. In practice, for a small outdoor herd, this is a brief annual conversation that sets up the prescription arrangement and covers the vaccination and worming programme. The main vaccines relevant to a small UK outdoor herd are erysipelas and parvovirus (usually given as a combined product) for sows and breeding animals. Weaners bought in for the freezer need only worming in most cases.

is a real risk in outdoor pigs on soil that has held pigs before. The standard programme is a primary course of two injections 3,4 weeks apart, then a booster every six months or before each farrowing in breeding animals. A gilt coming in from a new source should have her primary course on arrival, before she joins established stock.

Weaners bought from an outdoor herd should be wormed on arrival. A single dose of flubendazole in feed, or ivermectin by injection on prescription, covers roundworms and mange mites. Repeat at 8-week intervals if pigs are on ground that has held pigs in previous years. Keep the withdrawal period in mind, most worming products have a 14,28 day withdrawal for pork.

Every medicine given to a food-producing pig must be recorded: the pig or group ID, the date, the medicine name and batch number, the dose given, and the withdrawal period. Inspection of these records can happen during any APHA or Trading Standards visit. A simple paper book works fine; keep it with the medicines.
January: winter health check, body condition scoring all breeding animals
March: erysipelas booster for sows (if on a six-month cycle)
May/June: arriving weaners wormed on delivery; start of outdoor season check
September: erysipelas booster if on a six-month programme; check finishing pigs are on target weight for autumn
November: review medicine records with vet; confirm prescription renewals for next year
